sections.heading: Definition of Artificial Intelligence
sections.body: Artificial Intelligence is the branch of computer science concerned with building systems that can perform tasks which normally require human intelligence — perceiving, reasoning, learning, and deciding. Rather than following one rigid definition, AI is best understood as the pursuit of machines that act intelligently in their environment.

sections.heading: Goals of AI
sections.body: The core goals of AI research are to build systems that can reason and solve problems, represent and use knowledge, learn from experience, perceive their environment (through vision, speech, sensors), and act autonomously to achieve objectives. Different AI systems pursue different subsets of these goals depending on their purpose.

sections.heading: AI vs Human Intelligence
sections.body: Human intelligence is general, adaptive, and grounded in consciousness, emotion, and lived experience. Current AI systems, by contrast, are typically narrow — excelling at a specific task by processing far more data far faster than a person could, but without genuine understanding, common sense, or the ability to transfer skill across unrelated domains the way a human effortlessly can.

sections.heading: Branches of AI
sections.body: AI is an umbrella term covering many specialized branches: Machine Learning (systems that improve from data), Natural Language Processing (understanding and generating human language), Computer Vision (interpreting images and video), Robotics (physical agents acting in the world), Expert Systems (encoding specialist knowledge as rules), and Planning (sequencing actions to reach a goal).

sections.heading: AI Workflow
sections.body: Building an AI system typically follows a workflow: define the problem, collect and prepare data, choose or design a model/approach, train and tune it, evaluate its performance against real requirements, deploy it, and monitor it over time — since real-world conditions change and a model that worked yesterday can degrade tomorrow.

sections.heading: Weak AI vs Strong AI
sections.body: Weak AI (also called narrow AI) is designed to perform one task well — this describes every AI system in use today, from spam filters to voice assistants. Strong AI (or Artificial General Intelligence) refers to a hypothetical system with human-level, general-purpose intelligence across any task. Strong AI remains a long-term research goal, not something that exists in deployed systems.

quiz.question: Which of these best matches every AI system in use today?
Which is NOT typically listed as a core goal of AI?
Natural Language Processing is best described as:
options: A branch of AI focused on understanding and generating human language
A synonym for Artificial General Intelligence
A hardware requirement for all AI systems
